    By Content Type

    Content TypeEmailsTotal Sent Open RateClick RateCTOR

    Announcement

    Subject Line Patterns

    Smaller audience segments (under ~100 recipients) dominate the top open rates, making it difficult to isolate subject line style as the primary driver. That said, among emails with similar audience sizes, a few patterns emerge: (1) Urgency or action-oriented framing ('Action required', 'Coming soon', 'Upcoming changes') correlates with above-average open rates (43-62%) even at moderate list sizes (96-563). (2) 'Now live' phrasing appears in two emails with 43-44% open rates at 200+ recipients, suggesting launch announcements perform consistently. (3) The $43M funding announcement subject lines show dramatically varying open rates (8.4% to 33.3%) across different audience segments sent the same day, indicating audience targeting matters more than subject wording in this dataset. The data does not clearly support length as a differentiator — both short and longer subjects appear across high and low performers.

    CTA Patterns

    The data does not include body copy or explicit CTA text, so direct CTA analysis is not possible. However, click rate patterns offer a partial signal: emails framed around personal engagement ('Thank You + Quick Feedback Request': 58.8% click rate; 'Welcome + Member Kit': 37.9% click rate) drive far higher clicks than product feature announcements (most under 5%). Compliance-driven emails ('Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC': 2.1%) generate clicks despite not being high-urgency feature releases. Most 'New Analytic reports' emails had 0.0% click rates regardless of open rate, suggesting that report announcement emails do not drive action.

    Timing Patterns

    The data does not support a clear send-day or send-time pattern. Emails span multiple days of the week across 2025-2026 without consistent timing metadata (no send-time provided, only dates). High open rates appear across Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday sends with no discernible clustering. Insufficient data to draw timing conclusions.

    Data Note

    31 emails total, but audience sizes vary enormously — from 1 to 1,077 recipients. The top 3 open rates all come from very small lists (17, 96, and 29 recipients), where individual behavior has outsized influence on percentages. Several subject lines were sent to multiple different segments on the same or nearby dates (e.g., the $43M funding announcement, 'New Analytic reports' emails), creating quasi-A/B test conditions but with confounded audience differences. Patterns based on small-list emails should be treated as directional only.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do 96 61.5% 2.1%
    Submit multi-state enrollments easier with new license flexibility 245 48.6% 0.8%
    Coming soon: Standard payer names will be required for all enrollment records 244 48.1% 1.2%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding 146 46.6% 0.0%
    Manage terminations & billing with more clarity 94 45.7% 2.1%

    Subject Line Patterns

    Bracket-prefixed subjects with a clear event or asset type (e.g., '[You're invited]', '[Final chance for coffee]', '[Report]') dominate the top open rates, with the top two performers both using brackets. The coffee/casual meeting framing appears in 3 of the top 4 emails and correlates strongly with high opens (37.5–47.3%). Dollar-figure specificity ('[...costs some teams $1M+]') appears in the single highest-performing subject. Questions do not show a clear advantage over statements in this dataset. Urgency/scarcity language ('Final chance') correlates with the second-highest open rate. Larger list sends (3,000+) consistently underperform (6.6–16.5%), suggesting audience segmentation or fatigue may suppress opens regardless of subject quality, making subject-line-only attribution difficult. Pun-based subjects ('A latte teams are dealing with...') performed worst at 6.6% despite the largest send volume.

    CTA Patterns

    The email data does not include CTA text or body content, so direct CTA patterns cannot be determined. However, content themes in high-performing subject lines center on: (1) event invitations or informal meetings ('coffee break', 'you're invited'), (2) specific financial impact framing ('costs some teams $1M+'), and (3) named research assets ('[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment'). Click rates across all emails are low (0.0–2.8%) and do not correlate strongly with open rate — the '[Report]' email sent to 877 had the highest click rate (2.8%) at a moderate open rate (29.5%), suggesting report-gated content may drive clicks better than open-rate rank implies.

    Timing Patterns

    The data does not support a clear day-of-week or time-of-day pattern because send times are not provided, only dates. Looking at months: top-performing emails cluster in April–May 2026 and late October–November 2025. However, this may reflect audience segment differences and campaign topic rather than timing effects. The same date (2026-01-21) shows three sends of the same '[Report]' subject to different segments with open rates of 13.1%, 29.5%, and 34.1%, indicating segment quality likely drives performance more than timing. No reliable timing pattern can be extracted from this data alone.

    Data Note

    31 email sends analyzed, but many represent the same campaign sent to multiple segments on the same date (e.g., three '[Report]' sends on 2026-01-21; duplicate subject lines on 2025-08-21, 2025-07-10, etc.). Effective unique campaign count is lower (~18-20). Send volumes vary widely (65 to 3,852), making open rate comparisons unreliable across segments — small lists (65–144 sends) may show inflated or deflated rates due to small sample noise. Click rate data is present but body content and CTA text are absent, limiting content-level analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [You’re invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+) 1,124 47.3% 2.2%
    [Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue 998 44.3% 2.0%
    ☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue? 1,201 37.5% 2.4%
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 1,793 34.1% 2.5%

    Subject Line Patterns

    1) Highly specific, personalized context outperforms generic invitations: the two highest open rates (44.3% and 24.3%) came from emails referencing a named conference ('During JPM') or a unique experiential event ('Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience'), suggesting that specificity and novelty in the subject line drive opens. 2) Shorter, conversational subjects perform competitively: 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' (12.0% with 489 sent) outperformed many longer, more formal subjects, suggesting casual phrasing can work. 3) Urgency/scarcity language ('Final Seats Remaining', 'Last Call', 'Last Chance') does NOT consistently lift open rates — emails using these phrases cluster in the 3.7%–12.8% range with no clear advantage over non-urgency emails at similar list sizes. 4) The 'A Seat at the Table' framing appears three times with middling results (5.4%–12.2%), suggesting it is not a strong differentiator. 5) Small, targeted lists (under ~100 recipients) tend to show higher open rates, making subject line comparisons across different list sizes unreliable — the top two results were sent to 79 and 70 recipients respectively.

    CTA Patterns

    The data does not include email body copy or explicit CTA text, so direct CTA analysis is not possible. However, click rate patterns offer partial signal: the 'Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience' email (5.7% click rate) and the conference-tied 'APG Booth #519' email (4.3% click rate) had the highest click rates, suggesting that experiential or conference-anchored themes may drive more action than standard dinner invitations. Standard dinner invite emails generally produced click rates under 2%, and several high-open-rate emails had near-zero click rates (e.g., 44.3% open but 1.3% click for the JPM dinner to 79 recipients), indicating opens do not reliably convert to clicks in this dataset. Data is insufficient to draw conclusions about specific CTA wording.

    Timing Patterns

    The data does not support clear send-day or send-time patterns. Dates range from July 2025 to May 2026 and span various days of the week, but no consistent day-of-week or time-of-day metadata is provided. Higher open rates do not cluster around any visible date range. The highest open rate (44.3%) was a January send, but the second-highest (24.3%) was February, and other January/February sends performed in the mid-single digits. Insufficient data to draw timing conclusions.

    Data Note

    31 emails total, but list sizes vary enormously — from 23 to 1,466 recipients — making open rate comparisons across emails unreliable. The top two open rates came from sends of 79 and 70 recipients, where small absolute numbers of opens can produce outsized percentages. Patterns observed in small-list sends may not generalize to larger campaigns. No body copy, CTA text, send time, or audience segmentation data was available, limiting the depth of analysis possible.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 79 44.3% 1.3%
    In Vegas? Join us for Medallion’s Emerald Evening: A Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience 70 24.3% 5.7%
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 304 22.1% 3.4%
    Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston 1,463 12.8% 3.2%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 1,336 12.2% 0.4%

    Subject Line Patterns

    Personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject lines consistently outperform the branded 'Medallion Product Update - [Month Year]' format. Among emails with meaningful send volumes (100+), Roundup emails average ~36% open rate versus ~30% for Product Update emails. The possessive 'Your' and the sparkle emoji appear to correlate with higher engagement, though the difference narrows as list size grows. Shorter subjects (under 35 chars) like 'Your June Roundup ✨' also performed best, but the top result (52.2%) came from a send of only 23 recipients, limiting its reliability.

    CTA Patterns

    The email data provided does not include information about email body content, CTAs, or content themes. It is not possible to determine CTA or content patterns from subject lines and open/click rates alone. Click rates are universally low across all emails (0.3%–4.3%), with no consistent pattern tying a subject line format to higher click rates.

    Timing Patterns

    Emails were sent on varying days of the month (4th through 14th) with no clear day-of-week pattern discernible from the dates provided. Roundup emails were sent roughly on the first Tuesday or Wednesday of each month, and Product Update emails similarly in early month windows. No strong timing signal separates high from low performers — send volume and subject format appear to be larger factors than timing in this dataset.

    Data Note

    Only 12 emails analyzed, 11 of which had meaningful send volumes. The top-performing email ('Your June Roundup ✨') was sent to only 23 recipients, making its 52.2% open rate statistically unreliable. One email had a single recipient and 0% open rate and should be treated as a test send. The Roundup vs. Product Update comparison is partially confounded by list size — Roundup emails went to smaller lists (202–316), while Product Update emails went to much larger lists (1,260–1,385), which may independently affect open rates.

    Subject Line Patterns

    Top-performing subjects (open rates 25-38%) tend to use curiosity-gap or problem-framing language that names a pain point without fully resolving it (e.g., 'silent slowdown,' 'smarter onboarding'). Subjects starting with leading whitespace (\xa0) appear frequently across both high and low performers, so that character alone does not predict performance. Questions appear in mid-to-low performers ('Why re-verify...', 'What if credentialing files took 1 day instead of 1 week?'), suggesting questions do not clearly outperform statements in this dataset. Subjects that lead with a specific quantified claim ('Save up to 64%', '2x the volume', '1 day instead of 1 week') are associated with lower open rates (0-12.5%), though sample sizes are very small. No clear length-based pattern is supported given the noise and small samples.

    CTA Patterns

    Click rate data is insufficient to determine CTA or content patterns. Only one email shows a meaningful click rate ('The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about' at 18.2%), but it was sent to only 11 recipients, making it statistically unreliable. The top two emails by open rate had click rates of 3.5% and 2.7%. There is not enough data to identify what content themes or CTAs drive clicks.

    Timing Patterns

    Almost all emails were sent on 2025-12-09, with only two sent on 2025-12-10. No send-time data is available. There is no meaningful timing variation in this dataset to identify patterns. The two 2025-12-10 emails had open rates of 27.3% and 12.5%, which does not isolate a day-of-week effect given the confounding variables.

    Data Note

    Only 11 emails analyzed, with individual send volumes ranging from 3 to 37 recipients. Most sends are extremely small (3-11 recipients), making open and click rates highly volatile and statistically unreliable. Patterns should be treated as directional hypotheses only, not actionable conclusions. A minimum of several hundred recipients per variant would be needed to draw reliable inferences.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients

    Subject Line Patterns

    The top two performing subjects use concrete, specific language describing exactly what changed (e.g. naming the specific feature or report). The highest open rate (55.1%) came from a short, direct subject under 50 characters: 'New profile completion logic is now live'. The second-highest (51.1%) is much longer but still feature-specific. 'Is now live' or 'are now available' phrasing (confirming a release has happened) appears in 3 of the top 4 emails. The lowest performer (33.8%) shares an identical subject line with the #2 email, suggesting factors other than subject line alone drive opens. The data does not strongly support a length-based pattern given the small sample.

    CTA Patterns

    Click rate data is insufficient to determine CTA patterns. Only one email recorded any clicks (10.3% for 'New profile completion logic is now live'). The remaining four emails all show 0.0% click rate. This may indicate missing tracking data, no clickable links, or very low engagement with email body content across this category. No CTA content themes can be reliably identified from this data.

    Timing Patterns

    Emails were sent across July, August, and September 2025, but there are only 5 data points spread across different dates with no repeat send-days or times recorded. The identical subject line sent one week apart (Sep 10 vs Sep 17) showed a drop from 51.1% to 33.8%, possibly suggesting list fatigue or audience overlap, but this is speculative. No clear day-of-week or time-of-day pattern can be established from this data.

    Data Note

    Only 5 emails analyzed, with sent counts ranging from 74 to 180 recipients. This is a very small sample and patterns should be treated as directional at best. One subject line appears twice across different send dates, and four of five emails show 0% click rate which raises data quality questions around click tracking. No statistically significant conclusions should be drawn.

    A new approach to profile completion will go live soon ✅ 102 44.1% 0.0%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing 74 33.8% 0.0%

    Subject Line Patterns

    The data is too small (8 emails, 2 with zero sends) to draw strong conclusions, but among emails with meaningful send volumes: personalization combined with an incentive ('{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!') achieved the highest open rate at 19.7%. Notably, the similarly incentive-focused subject 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' sent to the largest list (10,608) performed worst at 5.0%, suggesting list quality or segmentation may matter more than subject line alone. Questions ('Share your experience with Medallion?') and urgency framing ('Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey') both performed comparably (~19%). No clear pattern distinguishes top from bottom performers on subject line structure alone given the confounding variable of list size and segmentation.

    CTA Patterns

    The email data does not include body copy or CTA text, so CTA patterns cannot be determined from this dataset. Click rates range from 0.5% to 3.0%, with the highest click rate (3.0%) on the personalized + $20 incentive email, suggesting monetary incentives may drive clicks, but the sample is too small to confirm this as a reliable pattern.

    Timing Patterns

    Emails were sent across September–October 2025 and June 2026, covering various days of the month. There are not enough data points across varied days or times to identify a meaningful send-day or send-time pattern. The two June 2026 emails with real sends (156 recipients) performed comparably to September sends, but the sample is far too small to draw timing conclusions.

    Data Note

    Only 8 emails total, 2 of which had zero sends and are excluded from analysis. List sizes vary enormously (95 to 10,608), which is a major confounding variable — open rates across very different audience sizes are not directly comparable. Patterns should be treated as directional hypotheses only, not reliable benchmarks.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Subject Line Patterns

    Several patterns correlate with higher open rates, though many top performers have very small send volumes that limit confidence: (1) Urgency and proximity language ('So Close', 'Last Chance', 'Less than a month away') appears in multiple high-performing subjects (64.6%, 53.6%, 48.8%), especially for the Elevate conference series. (2) Personal/relational framing ('Thank you for spending time with us', 'We missed you!') drives very high open rates (64.3%, 54.5%) but on tiny lists of 11-15. (3) 'On demand' or 'Watch on demand' framing performs well (66.7%, 44.0%, 40.2%), suggesting post-event availability resonates. (4) Shorter, direct subjects like 'We missed you!' and 'Demo Day available on demand' outperform long descriptive subjects. (5) Bracket-style prefixes like '[Watch on demand]' or '[You're invited]' show mixed results—moderate performers (32.8%–40.2%) but not top tier. (6) The lowest performers tend to be longer, more generic invitation subjects sent to large cold lists (10–19% open rates). (7) Personalization tokens appear in large-list sends with mediocre results (22.8%, 20.0%), offering no clear uplift. Overall pattern: urgency + short length + relational tone correlates with higher opens, but small-list sends dominate the top of the ranking, making it difficult to isolate subject line effects from audience warmth.

    CTA Patterns

    The data does not include explicit CTA text, so direct CTA analysis is not possible. However, content themes in high-performing emails include: (1) On-demand viewing offers ('Demo Day available on demand', 'Watch on demand') consistently yield high open AND click rates (26.7%, 20.0%, 9.8%), suggesting this content theme drives engagement beyond opens. (2) Incentive-based themes ('Claim your coffee kit', 'Claim Your Elevate Gift Box') show strong open rates (54.7%, 53.6%) with moderate click rates (2.2%, 32.1%—though the 32.1% is from a 56-person list). (3) Post-event follow-up emails ('Thank you for spending time with us', 'Thank you for being a part of Elevate 2025') perform well on opens but the click rates vary widely. (4) Large cold-list webinar invite emails consistently underperform on clicks (0.2%–1.8%). The data is insufficient to draw conclusions about specific CTA button text or placement.

    Timing Patterns

    The data does not support a clear send-day or send-time pattern. Dates are available but not send times, and the dataset spans multiple campaigns, audiences, and funnel stages, making date-based comparisons confounded. High-performing emails are scattered across months (August 2025, November 2025, May–June 2026). No consistent day-of-week or seasonal pattern is discernible from this data alone.

    Data Note

    Dataset contains 65 emails with send volumes ranging from 8 to 13,988. The top 6 emails by open rate were all sent to lists of 56 or fewer recipients, which likely reflects warm or highly targeted audiences rather than subject line quality alone—these results are not reliably generalizable. The one statistically meaningful high-open email is 'You're So Close—Elevate is less than a month away!' (n=2,010, 64.6%), which provides stronger signal. Several subject lines contain encoding artifacts (\xa0 prefixes) suggesting data quality issues in source export. Two emails share identical subject lines with very different open rates (e.g., 'Thank you for spending time with us': 64.3% on n=14 vs. 11.8% on n=93), confirming that audience segment drives results as much as subject line copy.
